1:
On Leviticus 23:1 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark the opening of the calendar of holy seasons.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the setting. Adam Clarke: here begin the feasts of the LORD — the Sabbath, Passover and unleavened bread, firstfruits, Pentecost, trumpets, atonement, and tabernacles. Charles Ellicott: the regulations about the holiness of the sanctuary, the priests, and the people are now followed by statutes about holy seasons. The Puritan Henry: only God’s appointment can make time holy, for he is the Lord of time.

The verse opens the chapter. God speaks to Moses to give the law of the appointed feasts. The calendar of worship is about to be set.

So the witnesses see God himself begin to ordain the sacred seasons of Israel’s year. The witnesses note the divine source of the whole calendar that follows: “the LORD spake unto Moses” — the feasts are not of human devising but of God’s appointing, and Moses receives them, as Gill observes, much about the same time as the foregoing laws of holiness. They mark Henry’s weighty observation that it is only God’s appointment that can make time holy; he is the Lord of time, who set its wheels going and sanctifies what portions of it he will, so that the holy days draw their holiness from his word, not from the day itself. They observe Ellicott’s note on the order of Leviticus: having regulated the holiness of the sanctuary, the sacrifices, the priests, and the people, the law now turns to the holiness of seasons, that time as well as place and person be consecrated to God. They observe Clarke’s preview of the feasts to come — the weekly Sabbath, then the yearly cycle of Passover and unleavened bread, firstfruits, Pentecost, trumpets, the day of atonement, and tabernacles — the whole rhythm of Israel’s worship. They note that God orders not only how his people shall worship but when. They observe that the chapter is a divine almanac of grace. The verse thus opens the calendar: God speaks the law of the feasts — addressed, in the next verse, to all Israel.
2:
On Leviticus 23:2 — “Speak unto the children of Israel… Concerning the feasts of the LORD, which ye shall proclaim to be holy convocations, even these are my feasts” — the witnesses mark the feasts as God’s, proclaimed as holy convocations.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the terms. Adam Clarke: the word moed is applied to any solemn anniversary recording great facts. Keil and Delitzsch: the feasts of the LORD, proclaimed to be holy convocations. Ellicott: addressed to the people, who were to keep them. Wesley: “ye shall proclaim” — cause to be proclaimed by the priests; “holy convocations,” days for assembling to God’s worship.

The verse names the feasts God’s own. They are to be proclaimed as holy convocations, gatherings for worship. The days belong to the LORD.

So the witnesses see the feasts claimed by God as his own and appointed as gatherings of his people for worship. The witnesses note the emphatic possessive: “even these are my feasts” — the LORD calls them his, set apart for his honour, kept by his command; they are not the people’s inventions but God’s appointments, and to be observed as such. They mark the term Clarke unfolds: moed, an appointed or solemn season, used of any anniversary by which great ecclesiastical or providential facts were recorded and kept in memory; the feasts are fixed times set in the year for remembering God’s acts. They observe the public proclaiming: “ye shall proclaim” — Wesley notes this was to be done by the priests, who caused the feasts to be announced; the people were to be summoned to them. They observe the nature of the days: “holy convocations” — Wesley and Ellicott explain these as days for the people to assemble together to God’s worship in a special manner; not private observances but corporate, called gatherings of the whole congregation. They note that the feasts are thus both holy (set apart for God) and convocations (calling the people together); worship is appointed, public, and communal. They observe that God would have his mercies remembered by an assembled people at set times. They note that the calendar binds Israel together in common worship. The verse thus names the feasts God’s own, proclaimed as holy convocations — the first of them, the next verse declares, the weekly Sabbath.
3:
On Leviticus 23:3 — “Six days shall work be done: but the seventh day is the sabbath of rest, an holy convocation; ye shall do no work therein: it is the sabbath of the LORD in all your dwellings” — the witnesses mark the weekly Sabbath set first.

The grammatical-historical commentators note its primacy. Adam Clarke: the Sabbath, as the first and greatest solemnity, is named first; he who kept not this could keep none of the others. Charles Ellicott: recurring weekly, the oldest and most important of all festivals, the Sabbath introduces the holy seasons. The Reformed Gill: six days lawful work may be done. Wesley: no work at all, as on the day of expiation.

The verse sets the Sabbath first. Six days for work, the seventh a holy rest in every dwelling. The weekly rest heads the calendar.

So the witnesses see the weekly Sabbath placed at the head of all the feasts, the foundation of Israel’s sacred time. The witnesses note the rhythm: “six days shall work be done: but the seventh day is the sabbath of rest” — the ordinary labour of six days is bounded by the holy rest of the seventh, the pattern set at creation (Genesis 2:3) and woven into the week. They mark the primacy the expositors give it: Clarke observes that the Sabbath, as the first and greatest solemnity, is named before all the yearly feasts; indeed, he adds, the man who would not keep this most religiously was not fit to keep any of the others, the weekly rest being the school of all the rest. Ellicott calls it the oldest and most important of the festivals, recurring every week, fitly set at the threshold of the holy seasons. They observe the strictness: “ye shall do no work therein” — Wesley notes this runs in the general, excluding all earthly employment of profit or pleasure, as on the day of atonement; the Sabbath rest is entire. They note the breadth: “in all your dwellings” — not only at the sanctuary but in every house, throughout the land, the Sabbath was to be kept; it was a rest unto the LORD wherever Israel dwelt. They observe that the holy convocation made it a day of worship as well as rest. They note that the yearly feasts are built upon the weekly. The verse thus sets the Sabbath first: the seventh-day rest in every dwelling — before the yearly feasts begin in the next verses.
4:
On Leviticus 23:4 — “These are the feasts of the LORD, even holy convocations, which ye shall proclaim in their seasons” — the witnesses mark the heading of the yearly feasts.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the new heading. Keil and Delitzsch: the special heading for the yearly feasts — “at their appointed time.” Charles Ellicott: the festivals proper, distinguished from the Sabbath, now enumerated in order. The Puritan Henry: called the feasts of the LORD because he appointed them; Jeroboam’s feast of his own heart (1 Kings 12:33) was an affront. The Reformed Calvin: the other festivals have an affinity to the Sabbath.

The verse heads the yearly feasts. They are the LORD’s, proclaimed in their seasons. The annual cycle now begins.

So the witnesses see a fresh heading set over the yearly feasts, distinct from the weekly Sabbath just named. The witnesses note the renewed claim: “these are the feasts of the LORD” — Henry stresses that they are called his because he appointed them; and he sets against them the warning of Jeroboam, who devised a feast of his own heart (1 Kings 12:33), an affront to God and a reproach to the people — will-worship, however solemn, is no true feast of the LORD. They mark the appointed times: “which ye shall proclaim in their seasons” — Keil and Delitzsch render it “at their appointed time”; each feast had its fixed place in the year, and was to be kept neither sooner nor later, but in the season God had set. They observe Ellicott’s note that this introduces the festivals proper, as distinguished from the Sabbath (which recurs weekly), now to be enumerated in their regular order through the year. They observe Calvin’s remark that all these other festivals have an affinity to the Sabbath, partaking of its character of holy rest and consecrated time, the yearly feasts being as it were greater Sabbaths. They note that the feasts are God’s, fixed by him, kept in his seasons; man may neither invent his own holy days nor shift God’s. They observe that the worship of God is to be regulated by his word, in time as in all else. The verse thus heads the yearly feasts: the LORD’s, proclaimed in their seasons — beginning, in the next verse, with the Passover.
5:
On Leviticus 23:5 — “In the fourteenth day of the first month at even is the LORD’s passover” — the witnesses mark the Passover, first of the yearly feasts.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the date. Keil and Delitzsch: the leading directions for the Passover are repeated from Exodus 12:6. Adam Clarke refers to the fuller account at Exodus 12:21. Charles Ellicott: the first month, Abib in the Pentateuch, Nisan in the later books; the fourteenth at even. The Reformed Gill: Nisan, the month of the Exodus, made the first.

The verse sets the Passover. On the fourteenth of the first month at even is the LORD’s passover. The redemption-feast opens the year.

So the witnesses see the yearly cycle open with the Passover, the feast of redemption, on the evening of the fourteenth of the first month. The witnesses note the date: “the fourteenth day of the first month at even” — Ellicott observes that this month is called Abib in the Pentateuch and Nisan in the later Scriptures; Gill marks it as the month of the Exodus, for that cause made the first of the year (Exodus 12:2). They mark that this is a brief restatement, the full institution having been given in Exodus: Keil and Delitzsch note that the leading directions for the Passover and unleavened bread are here repeated from Exodus 12:6 and following, and Clarke simply refers the reader to that larger account. They observe the place of the Passover at the head of the year’s feasts: as redemption from Egypt was the foundation of Israel’s national and religious life, so the feast that commemorates it stands first among the yearly solemnities. They observe the typology the witnesses carry from Exodus: the Passover lamb prefigures Christ, “our passover… sacrificed for us” (1 Corinthians 5:7), the year of grace opening, as Israel’s did, with the blood of the Lamb. They note that the feast is the LORD’s, kept at his appointed evening. They observe that all the other feasts flow from this one of redemption. The verse thus sets the Passover: the fourteenth at even — followed, in the next verse, by the feast of unleavened bread.
6:
On Leviticus 23:6 — “And on the fifteenth day of the same month is the feast of unleavened bread unto the LORD: seven days ye must eat unleavened bread” — the witnesses mark the feast of unleavened bread.

The grammatical-historical commentators note its joining to the Passover. Keil and Delitzsch: on the fifteenth, the feast of unleavened bread, seven days. Charles Ellicott refers to Exodus 12:15. The Reformed Gill: the fifteenth, the day they went out of Egypt with their dough unleavened, having no time to leaven it.

The verse adds the unleavened bread. On the fifteenth begins the seven-day feast. The Passover passes into the week of unleavened bread.

So the witnesses see the Passover joined to the seven-day feast of unleavened bread, beginning the day after. The witnesses note the date: “on the fifteenth day of the same month” — the feast of unleavened bread opens the morning after the paschal evening, and runs seven days. They mark Gill’s reminder of its origin: the fifteenth was the very day Israel went out of Egypt, carrying their dough still unleavened, for they had no time to leaven it in their haste; the feast commemorates that hurried departure. They observe the requirement: “seven days ye must eat unleavened bread” — for a full week no leaven was to be eaten, the directions, as Ellicott notes, given fully in Exodus 12:15 and following. They observe the typology the witnesses draw from Exodus and the apostle: leaven, which spreads and corrupts, is a figure of sin and of the “old leaven” of malice and wickedness, to be purged out, that the redeemed keep the feast with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth (1 Corinthians 5:8). They note the close bond of the two feasts: redemption (the Passover) issues at once in holiness (the unleavened bread); the people saved by the blood are to live a purged life. They observe that the seven days, a complete period, signify a whole life cleansed. They note that the feast is “unto the LORD,” kept to his honour. The verse thus adds the unleavened bread: seven days from the fifteenth — the first day, the next verse says, a holy convocation.
7:
On Leviticus 23:7 — “In the first day ye shall have an holy convocation: ye shall do no servile work therein” — the witnesses mark the first day’s holy convocation.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the first of the seven days, the fifteenth of Nisan; “servile work” defined during the second Temple as building, pulling down, and the like (Exodus 12:16). The Reformed Gill: the first of the seven days, set apart from the rest as a holy convocation.

The verse hallows the first day. It is a holy convocation, free of servile work. The week of unleavened bread opens with a sacred assembly.

So the witnesses see the first day of the unleavened-bread week set apart as a holy assembly with rest from common labour. The witnesses note the sanctity of the day: “in the first day ye shall have an holy convocation” — the fifteenth of Nisan, Gill observes, was distinguished from the other days of the festival as a day for the people to be called together to God’s worship. They mark the kind of rest commanded: “ye shall do no servile work therein” — not the entire cessation of the weekly Sabbath and the day of atonement, but abstinence from servile or laborious work; Ellicott notes that during the second Temple “servile work” was defined to consist in building, pulling down, and such heavy toil, while the necessary preparing of food was allowed (Exodus 12:16). They observe the distinction the witnesses keep between the festival days, on which servile work alone was forbidden, and the Sabbath and atonement, on which no work at all might be done; the feast days permitted the dressing of food and the like. They note that the holy convocation made the day one of worship, the people gathered before God. They observe that the week of holiness thus opened and (as the eighth day shows) closed with a sacred assembly, the joyful days framed by worship. They note that rest from toil freed the people for the things of God. The verse thus hallows the first day: a holy convocation, free of servile work — the daily offerings of the week following in the next verse.
8:
On Leviticus 23:8 — “But ye shall offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD seven days: in the seventh day is an holy convocation: ye shall do no servile work therein” — the witnesses mark the seven days’ offerings and the seventh-day convocation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the sacrifices. Charles Ellicott: in addition to the daily offerings, on each of the seven days were offered two young bullocks, a ram, and seven lambs, with meat and drink offerings, and a goat for a sin offering (Numbers 28:19). The Reformed Gill: a burnt offering on each of the seven days.

The verse appoints the offerings. Fire offerings for seven days, the seventh a holy convocation. The feast is bounded by sacrifice and assembly.

So the witnesses see the week of unleavened bread filled with daily sacrifice and closed, like its opening, with a holy convocation. The witnesses note the offerings: “ye shall offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD seven days” — on every one of the seven days, besides the continual daily sacrifice, a special burnt offering was made; Ellicott and Gill detail it from Numbers as two young bullocks, one ram, and seven lambs, with their meat and drink offerings, and a goat for a sin offering (Numbers 28:19). They mark that the feast was not idle rejoicing but a week of worship sustained by sacrifice; every day brought its offering to the LORD. They observe the closing convocation: “in the seventh day is an holy convocation: ye shall do no servile work therein” — the last of the seven days, like the first, was a sacred assembly with rest from servile labour, so that the week was framed at both ends by holy days. They note the symmetry the witnesses observe: the feast opens and closes with a convocation, the joyful eating of unleavened bread bracketed by worship and rest. They observe that the sin offering each day kept the people mindful that even their feasts needed atonement; joy and the sense of sin were held together. They note that the whole week was thus consecrated, beginning to end. They observe that sacrifice was the heart of the feast. The verse thus appoints the offerings: fire offerings for seven days, the seventh a convocation — the law then turning, in the next verses, to the sheaf of firstfruits.
9:
On Leviticus 23:9 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark the new word introducing the firstfruits.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the celebration of the sheaf of firstfruits formed no part of the original Passover institution (Exodus 12:1), and the omer ritual could not be kept in the wilderness; hence a separate divine communication introduces it.

The verse opens a new precept. God speaks again to give the law of the firstfruits sheaf. A fresh ordinance begins.

So the witnesses see a fresh divine word open the law of the wave sheaf of firstfruits, distinct from the Passover ordinances just rehearsed. The witnesses note the separate formula: “the LORD spake unto Moses” — the repeated preface marks off the firstfruits as a new and distinct communication, not merely a continuation of the unleavened-bread law. They mark Ellicott’s observation of why it stands apart: the celebration of the sheaf of firstfruits formed no part of the original institution of the Passover in Egypt (Exodus 12:1), for it belonged to harvest, and the omer ritual could not be observed in the wilderness, where there was no sowing or reaping; it was given prospectively, against the time they should come into the land and reap its grain. They observe that the law is thus forward-looking: God ordains beforehand how Israel shall acknowledge him as the giver of the harvest, once the wilderness is past and the fields are theirs. They note that the firstfruits feast, falling within the days of unleavened bread, binds the redemption of the Passover to the provision of the harvest; the God who saved them also feeds them. They observe the typological weight the witnesses will draw out: this sheaf of firstfruits, waved before the LORD, prefigures Christ risen, “the firstfruits of them that slept” (1 Corinthians 15:20). They note that God appoints even the manner of thanksgiving for the crops. The verse thus opens the new precept: the LORD speaks the law of firstfruits — the offering itself commanded in the next verse.

So the witnesses see a fresh divine word open the law of the wave sheaf of firstfruits. They note that the repeated preface marks the firstfruits as a new and distinct ordinance, not merely a continuation of the unleavened-bread law. They mark Ellicott’s observation of why it stands apart: the celebration of the sheaf of firstfruits formed no part of the original institution of the Passover in Egypt, for it belonged to the harvest, and the omer ritual could not be observed in the wilderness, where there was no sowing or reaping; it was given prospectively, against the time they should come into the land and reap its grain. They observe that the law is forward-looking, ordaining beforehand how Israel shall acknowledge God as the giver of the harvest once the wilderness is past. They note that the firstfruits feast, falling within the days of unleavened bread, binds the redemption of the Passover to the provision of the harvest: the God who saved them also feeds them. They observe the typological weight the witnesses will draw out, that this sheaf, waved before the LORD, prefigures Christ risen, “the firstfruits of them that slept” (1 Corinthians 15:20).
10:
On Leviticus 23:10 — “When ye be come into the land… and shall reap the harvest thereof, then ye shall bring a sheaf of the firstfruits of your harvest unto the priest” — the witnesses mark the sheaf of firstfruits.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the third of four instances in Leviticus where a law is given prospectively, for the land (Leviticus 19:23). The Reformed Calvin: rules for the second day of the festival, dedicated to the firstfruits; a handful delivered to the priest. Wesley: an omer, not in the ear, but purged, dried, and beaten out (Leviticus 2:14).

The verse commands the sheaf. When they reap in the land, they bring the firstfruits sheaf to the priest. The first of the harvest is given to God.

So the witnesses see Israel commanded, once settled in the land, to bring the first sheaf of the harvest to the priest before using the crop. The witnesses note the forward look: “when ye be come into the land… and shall reap the harvest” — Ellicott marks this as one of several laws in Leviticus given prospectively, having no immediate bearing in the wilderness but binding when they should possess and till the land (Leviticus 19:23). They mark the offering: “a sheaf of the firstfruits of your harvest” — the first ripe grain (barley, the earliest crop) was to be brought to the priest; Wesley notes, from Leviticus 2:14 and Josephus, that it was not offered in the ear or as a rough handful, but the grain purged from the chaff, dried, and beaten out, an omer of prepared corn. They observe Calvin’s placing of the rite on the day after the first holy convocation, dedicated to the firstfruits, a handful delivered into the priest’s hand. They note the principle the witnesses draw: God, the giver of every blessing, is to be acknowledged with the first and best of the harvest before the people take their own use of it; the firstfruits hallow the whole crop. They observe the typology pressed throughout: this firstfruits sheaf prefigures Christ, “the firstfruits of them that slept” (1 Corinthians 15:20), raised and presented to God as the pledge of the whole harvest of the resurrection. They note that the first of all belongs to God. The verse thus commands the sheaf: the firstfruits brought to the priest — to be waved, the next verse says, before the LORD.
11:
On Leviticus 23:11 — “And he shall wave the sheaf before the LORD, to be accepted for you: on the morrow after the sabbath the priest shall wave it” — the witnesses mark the waving of the sheaf.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rite. Adam Clarke: the priest moved it to and fro before the people, calling their attention to God’s providence and exciting gratitude. Charles Ellicott: he mixed the omer of meal with oil and frankincense, waved it, and burnt a handful (Leviticus 2:15). The Reformed Gill and Wesley: waved in the name of the whole congregation, sanctifying the whole harvest.

The verse directs the waving. The priest waves the sheaf before the LORD, to be accepted for them. The firstfruits are presented and the harvest hallowed.

So the witnesses see the firstfruits sheaf waved before the LORD as the accepted offering of the whole people. The witnesses note the act: “he shall wave the sheaf before the LORD” — Clarke explains the waving as a moving of the sheaf to and fro before the people, calling their attention to the work of divine providence in preserving the kindly fruits of the earth, and stirring up their gratitude to God. They mark the manner Ellicott details: the priest mixed the omer of meal with a log of oil and a handful of frankincense, as with other meat offerings, waved it, and burnt a handful upon the altar (Leviticus 2:15). They observe the representative character: “to be accepted for you” — Wesley and Gill note the priest waved it in the name of the whole congregation, so that this one sheaf as it were sanctified to them the entire harvest and gave them comfortable use of all the rest; the part offered hallowed the whole. They observe the time, which the next clause fixes and the witnesses will discuss: “on the morrow after the sabbath.” They note the typology pressed strongly here: the sheaf waved “to be accepted for you” prefigures Christ risen and presented before God, accepted on behalf of his people, the firstfruits guaranteeing their harvest (1 Corinthians 15:23). They observe that the believer may eat his bread with joy when God has first had his due. They note that acceptance flows from the offering presented. The verse thus directs the waving: the sheaf presented, the harvest hallowed — the day of it, “the morrow after the sabbath,” explained in the verses that follow.
12:
On Leviticus 23:12 — “And ye shall offer that day when ye wave the sheaf an he lamb without blemish of the first year for a burnt offering unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the burnt offering with the sheaf.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: with the omer of firstfruits a lamb was offered, besides the feast sacrifices of Leviticus 23:8. The Reformed Gill: besides the daily morning and evening sacrifice and the additional offerings of the seven days.

The verse adds a lamb. With the waved sheaf an unblemished lamb is offered as a burnt offering. The thanksgiving is joined to sacrifice.

So the witnesses see the offering of the firstfruits sheaf accompanied by the sacrifice of an unblemished lamb. The witnesses note the addition: “ye shall offer that day… an he lamb without blemish of the first year for a burnt offering” — on the very day the sheaf was waved, a perfect male lamb was to be offered whole to the LORD as a burnt offering; Ellicott and Gill observe that this was over and above the daily morning and evening sacrifice and the special offerings appointed for the seven days of the feast (Leviticus 23:8). They mark the fitness of joining a burnt offering to the firstfruits: the burnt offering, wholly consumed and ascending to God, expressed entire consecration and thanksgiving; the people gave back to God not only the firstfruits of their grain but a whole victim, owning him as Lord of all. They observe the requirement of perfection: “without blemish… of the first year” — the lamb, like every acceptable sacrifice, must be sound and unflawed, fit for God’s altar. They note the typology the witnesses carry: the spotless lamb burnt with the firstfruits points, as ever, to Christ, the unblemished offering through whom alone the firstfruits and the harvest are accepted. They observe that thanksgiving for the harvest was not offered apart from atoning and consecrating sacrifice; gratitude came clothed in blood. They note that God is acknowledged both as provider and as the one to whom all is due. The verse thus adds the lamb: a burnt offering with the sheaf — its meat and drink offering specified in the next verse.
13:
On Leviticus 23:13 — “And the meat offering thereof shall be two tenth deals of fine flour mingled with oil… and the drink offering thereof shall be of wine, the fourth part of an hin” — the witnesses mark the doubled meat offering.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: ordinarily only one tenth deal of flour was required for a meat offering; here it is doubled, to exhibit the plentiful harvest. The Reformed Gill notes the Jewish reasons. Wesley: two tenth deals, that is two omers, whereas other lamb sacrifices took but one; one part for the lamb’s offering, one in acknowledgment of the harvest.

The verse doubles the meat offering. Two tenths of flour with oil, and a drink offering of wine. The plenty of harvest is owned in the increase.

So the witnesses see the meat offering with the firstfruits doubled beyond the usual measure, in token of the harvest’s plenty. The witnesses note the unusual quantity: “two tenth deals of fine flour mingled with oil” — Ellicott observes that ordinarily only one tenth deal of fine flour accompanied the offering of a lamb (Numbers 28:9), but here the measure is doubled. They mark the reason the witnesses give: the doubling exhibited the plentifulness of the harvest now begun, the increased meat offering owning the increased bounty of God; Wesley adds the distinction that one tenth deal was the ordinary measure due with the lamb, and the second was added in special acknowledgment of the harvest, so that the very quantity preached gratitude for the crop. They observe the drink offering: “the fourth part of an hin” of wine, poured out with the rest, as the appointed libation. They note that the firstfruits offering thus gathered grain, oil, and wine — the staple fruits of the land — into one act of thanksgiving, all given back to God who gave them. They observe that the increase of the offering matched the increase of the field; God is honoured with abundance from his abundance. They note that the meat and drink offerings turned the firstfruits into a full and joyful thank-offering. They observe that gratitude is measured to the mercy received. The verse thus doubles the meat offering: two tenths of flour, with wine — before the law forbids, in the next verse, the eating of the new corn until this is done.
14:
On Leviticus 23:14 — “And ye shall eat neither bread, nor parched corn, nor green ears, until the selfsame day that ye have brought an offering unto your God” — the witnesses mark the firstfruits given before the harvest is used.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rule. Adam Clarke: it is right that God, the dispenser of every blessing, be acknowledged, the firstfruits dedicated to him. Charles Ellicott: the Israelites were not to taste the new harvest till they had dedicated the firstfruit. The Reformed Gill: not to make bread of the new corn, nor eat parched corn or green ears, before the offering. Wesley: bread of new wheat, green ears, usual food.

The verse forbids the new corn first. None may eat of the new harvest until the firstfruits are offered. God has his portion first.

So the witnesses see the people forbidden to taste the new harvest until God has received the firstfruits. The witnesses note the prohibition: “ye shall eat neither bread, nor parched corn, nor green ears” — until the firstfruits sheaf was offered, no one might eat of the new grain in any form, whether baked into bread, parched, or plucked green from the ear; Gill and Wesley note these were the common ways of eating new corn. They mark the binding condition: “until the selfsame day that ye have brought an offering unto your God” — only when the firstfruits had been presented might the people use the rest of the crop; God’s portion came first. They observe the principle the witnesses draw out: Clarke and Ellicott press that it is right and fitting that God, the dispenser of every blessing and the giver of the harvest, be acknowledged with the firstfruits before his creatures take their own enjoyment of it; to eat first and offer after would be to forget the giver in the gift. They note the discipline of patience and reverence in the rule: hungry though they might be for the new bread after the lean weeks before harvest, they must wait until God was honoured. They observe the typology the witnesses carry: as the firstfruits must be offered before the harvest is enjoyed, so Christ the firstfruits must be presented and accepted before his people enter their inheritance; the order is fixed. They note that all our use of God’s gifts should begin with thanksgiving to him. The verse thus forbids the new corn first: God’s firstfruits before the harvest’s use — this being “a statute for ever,” the law turns next to the counting toward Pentecost.
15:
On Leviticus 23:15 — “And ye shall count unto you from the morrow after the sabbath, from the day that ye brought the sheaf of the wave offering; seven sabbaths shall be complete” — the witnesses mark the counting toward Pentecost, with a disputed “sabbath.”

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the day. Adam Clarke: from the sixteenth of the first month to the sixth of the third; the seven weeks complete. Charles Ellicott: from the day after the first day of holy convocation (a rest day), the fifteenth, so from the sixteenth. The Puritan Henry: the feast of weeks, fifty days after the passover. Wesley: from the sixteenth, the second day of unleavened bread.

The verse begins the count. From the morrow after the sabbath, seven full weeks are counted. The way to Pentecost is measured out.

So the witnesses see the counting of seven weeks begun from the day of the wave sheaf, leading on to Pentecost. The witnesses note the command: “ye shall count unto you… seven sabbaths shall be complete” — seven full weeks, forty-nine days, were to be numbered from the offering of the firstfruits sheaf, the fiftieth day being the feast of weeks. They mark the disputed phrase “the morrow after the sabbath,” on which the expositors here agree against the Sadducean reading. Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, and Wesley take “the sabbath” to mean not the weekly seventh-day Sabbath but the first day of unleavened bread (the fifteenth of Nisan), which was kept as a sabbath or rest-day; so the count began on the sixteenth, the morrow after that holy convocation, and ran to the sixth of the third month. They observe the names of the feast Henry recalls: the feast of weeks (for the seven weeks counted), of harvest, and, in the New Testament, Pentecost (from the fifty days). They note the careful, deliberate counting: Israel was to number each day from the firstfruits to the full harvest feast, keeping the season in view. They observe the typology the witnesses draw: from the firstfruits (Christ’s resurrection) fifty days are counted to Pentecost, when the Spirit was poured out (Acts 2:1); the wave sheaf and the loaves are linked across the seven weeks as the resurrection and the gift of the Spirit. They note that the people were taught to look from one mercy to the next. The verse thus begins the count: seven complete weeks from the wave sheaf — to the fiftieth day, named in the next verse.
16:
On Leviticus 23:16 — “Even unto the morrow after the seventh sabbath shall ye number fifty days; and ye shall offer a new meat offering unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the fiftieth day and the new offering.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the day. Keil and Delitzsch: even unto the morrow after the seventh sabbath, fifty days; a new meat offering. Charles Ellicott: the day after the seven complete weeks, the fiftieth day — hence Pentecost (Acts 2:1). The Reformed Gill: the fiftieth day, or Pentecost. Wesley: a new meal offering of new corn made into loaves.

The verse names the fiftieth day. The count reaches fifty, with a new meat offering. Pentecost arrives with the new bread.

So the witnesses see the count completed at the fiftieth day, when a new offering of the new harvest was brought. The witnesses note the reckoning: “even unto the morrow after the seventh sabbath shall ye number fifty days” — after seven complete weeks (forty-nine days) from the wave sheaf, the next day, the fiftieth, was the feast; Ellicott observes that from this number comes its New Testament name, Pentecost, the “fiftieth-day” feast (Acts 2:1). They mark the offering that crowned it: “ye shall offer a new meat offering unto the LORD” — a new meal offering, made (as Wesley notes) of the new corn baked into loaves; whereas the firstfruits sheaf at Passover was of barley, the firstfruits at Pentecost were of the later wheat harvest, now ripe. They observe the symmetry the witnesses see: the firstfruits sheaf opened the harvest and the two loaves at Pentecost closed it, the whole grain harvest framed by thanksgiving to God. They note the typology carried through: as the wave sheaf figured Christ risen, so the fiftieth day figures the Pentecost of the Spirit (Acts 2:1), the new offering of loaves answering to the church gathered and presented to God as firstfruits of his new creation. They observe that the “new” offering marked a new stage of the harvest and, in the type, a new dispensation of grace. They note that God is owned at the harvest’s end as at its beginning. The verse thus names the fiftieth day: Pentecost with its new offering — the two wave loaves commanded in the next verse.
17:
On Leviticus 23:17 — “Ye shall bring out of your habitations two wave loaves of two tenth deals: they shall be of fine flour; they shall be baken with leaven; they are the firstfruits unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the two leavened loaves.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the loaves. Charles Ellicott: brought from the land of their habitations, Palestine (Numbers 15:2); two loaves of fine flour, baked with leaven. The Reformed Gill: out of their dwellings in Canaan, not from abroad; the firstfruits of the wheat harvest.

The verse commands the loaves. Two loaves of fine flour, baked with leaven, are the firstfruits. The bread of the people’s tables is offered.

So the witnesses see the firstfruits of Pentecost brought as two baked loaves, and these, unusually, leavened. The witnesses note the offering: “ye shall bring out of your habitations two wave loaves… they are the firstfruits unto the LORD” — from their own dwellings in the land (Gill and Ellicott specify Canaan, the loaves to be of home-grown grain, not foreign) the people brought two loaves of fine flour, the firstfruits of the wheat harvest, to be waved before the LORD. They mark the striking detail the witnesses observe: “they shall be baken with leaven” — whereas leaven was generally excluded from offerings made by fire upon the altar (Leviticus 2:11), these Pentecost loaves were expressly to be leavened; for they represented not the unleavened bread of the Exodus haste but the ordinary daily bread of the people’s tables, the common food of settled life, now offered to God. They observe that, being leavened, they were waved before the LORD and given to the priests, but not burnt on the altar, leaven being still unfit for the fire. They note the typology some of the witnesses draw: the two leavened loaves, made of the finest flour yet with leaven, may figure the people of God gathered at Pentecost — a redeemed people, the church, real firstfruits unto God, yet not sinless, having still the leaven of remaining corruption, accepted not in their own perfection but through the sacrifices offered with them. They note that even the common bread of life is to be hallowed to God. The verse thus commands the loaves: two leavened firstfruits — with their accompanying sacrifices, in the next verses.
18:
On Leviticus 23:18 — “And ye shall offer with the bread seven lambs without blemish of the first year, and one young bullock, and two rams… for a burnt offering unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the sacrifices with the loaves.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the additional sacrifices were two bullocks, one ram, and seven lambs for a burnt offering, and a goat for a sin offering (Numbers 28:27). Wesley notes the apparent difference from Numbers (“one bullock and two rams” here, “two bullocks and one ram” there), and resolves it.

The verse adds the burnt offerings. Lambs, a bullock, and rams are offered whole with the loaves. The thanksgiving is sealed with sacrifice.

So the witnesses see the Pentecost loaves accompanied by burnt offerings ascending whole to God. The witnesses note the sacrifices named here: “seven lambs without blemish of the first year, and one young bullock, and two rams” — offered with the bread as a burnt offering, with their meat and drink offerings, a sweet savour unto the LORD. They mark the apparent discrepancy with the parallel list in Numbers, which the witnesses face honestly: here the burnt offering is one bullock and two rams, while Numbers 28:27 names two bullocks and one ram. Wesley resolves it by supposing either that some liberty was allowed in the choice, or, more likely, that the two passages name different sets of offerings proper to the day, one of the bullocks and one of the rams being peculiar to each account, so that the totals are complementary rather than contradictory; Ellicott reckons the full provision of the day at two bullocks, one ram, and seven lambs, with a goat for a sin offering. They observe that the loaves, being leavened, could not be burnt, and so a full complement of unblemished burnt offerings was added, that the feast might be honoured with sacrifice ascending to God. They note that the firstfruits loaves were thus presented in the company of atoning and consecrating blood, accepted not alone but with the offerings. They observe that the spotless victims point, as ever, to Christ. The verse thus adds the burnt offerings: lambs, bullock, and rams with the bread — the sin and peace offerings following in the next verse.
19:
On Leviticus 23:19 — “Then ye shall sacrifice one kid of the goats for a sin offering, and two lambs of the first year for a sacrifice of peace offerings” — the witnesses mark the sin and peace offerings.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: a kid for a sin offering, and two lambs for peace offerings. Charles Ellicott notes the goat for sin and the two lambs for peace. The Reformed Gill: the sin offering for the whole congregation, typical of Christ, whose soul was made an offering for sin. Wesley: a kid here, not a bullock as in Leviticus 4:14, this being for sin in general, not a particular sin.

The verse adds sin and peace offerings. A goat for sin, two lambs for peace, complete the day. Atonement and communion are joined to thanksgiving.

So the witnesses see the Pentecost offerings completed with a sin offering and peace offerings, joining atonement and communion to the harvest thanksgiving. The witnesses note the sin offering: “one kid of the goats for a sin offering” — Gill observes this was for the sin of the whole congregation, typical of Christ, whose soul was made an offering for sin, and in virtue of whom all other offerings are accepted. They mark Wesley’s note on the kind of victim: a kid is appointed here, where Leviticus 4:14 required a bullock for the people’s sin; the difference being that the bullock was for some particular gross sin of the congregation, while this kid was a general sin offering proper to the feast, owning the people’s sinfulness even in their rejoicing. They observe the peace offerings: “two lambs… for a sacrifice of peace offerings” — the peace or thank offering, of which the offerers partook, expressed fellowship and communion with God, a shared feast of reconciliation. They note the fulness of the day’s worship: burnt offerings of entire consecration, a sin offering of atonement, and peace offerings of communion, all brought with the firstfruits loaves; the people drew near to God in every aspect of sacrifice. They observe that even at the joyful harvest feast, atonement was not omitted; thanksgiving rested on a sin offering. They note the typology: Christ is at once the burnt offering, the sin offering, and the peace; all the sacrifices meet in him. The verse thus adds the sin and peace offerings: atonement and communion with the feast — the waving of them with the loaves following in the next verse.
20:
On Leviticus 23:20 — “And the priest shall wave them with the bread of the firstfruits for a wave offering before the LORD… they shall be holy to the LORD for the priest” — the witnesses mark the waving and the priest’s portion.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: in the second Temple the two lambs were brought and waved, alive, together with the loaves. The Reformed Gill: the two wave loaves with the two lambs of the peace offerings, waved before the LORD. Wesley: some part of them waved in the name of the whole; for the priests, who had more than their usual portion.

The verse waves the loaves and lambs. The priest waves them before the LORD; they are holy for the priest. The firstfruits are presented and the priest fed.

So the witnesses see the loaves and the peace-offering lambs waved together before the LORD and assigned to the priests. The witnesses note the waving: “the priest shall wave them with the bread of the firstfruits for a wave offering” — the two loaves and the two lambs of the peace offering were presented together with the ceremony of waving before the LORD; Ellicott records that in the second Temple the lambs were brought and waved while yet alive, together with the bread. They mark Wesley’s practical note that some part, or the offerings in token, was waved in the name of the whole congregation, the loaves and lambs being too large and heavy to be literally moved about; the waving signified their dedication to God. They observe the disposal: “they shall be holy to the LORD for the priest” — the loaves and the flesh of the peace-offering lambs, once waved, became holy and were given to the priests for their portion; Wesley notes the priest here received more than his usual share (the breast and shoulder of ordinary peace offerings), the whole being his on this day. They note the fitness: the firstfruits, dedicated to God, were assigned to those who ministered before him, so that the priests lived from the offerings of the people. They observe that what is given to God is given to his servants who serve at the altar (1 Corinthians 9:13). They note that the firstfruits thus passed from the people, through God, to the priesthood. The verse thus waves the loaves and lambs: holy to the LORD for the priest — before the day is proclaimed, in the next verse, a holy convocation.
21:
On Leviticus 23:21 — “And ye shall proclaim on the selfsame day, that it may be an holy convocation unto you: ye shall do no servile work therein: it shall be a statute for ever” — the witnesses mark Pentecost a holy convocation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the proclaiming. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott: the proclamation made to the people by the priest with trumpet blasts; no servile work; a statute for ever (Leviticus 23:7). The Reformed Gill: the priests proclaimed it with the trumpet. Wesley: a sabbath of rest, instituted partly in memory of the giving of the law at Sinai.

The verse hallows Pentecost. The day is proclaimed a holy convocation, free of servile work. The feast of weeks is a day of rest and worship.

So the witnesses see the day of Pentecost proclaimed and kept as a holy convocation, like the feast days before it. The witnesses note the proclaiming: “ye shall proclaim on the selfsame day” — Gill, Ellicott, and Keil note this was announced to the people by the priests with blasts of the trumpet, that the fiftieth day be observed as a sacred assembly. They mark the rest commanded: “ye shall do no servile work therein” — as on the other festival days, ordinary laborious work was laid aside, though (as before) the needful preparing of food was allowed; the day was freed for worship. They observe Wesley’s note on the meaning the day gathered: besides being the feast of harvest firstfruits, Pentecost was held to commemorate the giving of the law at Sinai, which fell about fifty days after the Exodus; the feast thus joined the firstfruits of the field to the founding of the covenant nation at the mount. They observe the perpetuity: “a statute for ever in all your dwellings” — binding throughout their generations and wherever they lived. They note the typology the witnesses carry: this fiftieth-day convocation finds its great fulfilment in the Christian Pentecost, when, fifty days after the resurrection, the Spirit was given and the church gathered as God’s firstfruits (Acts 2:1). They note that the harvest of grain and the harvest of souls meet in the day. The verse thus hallows Pentecost: a holy convocation for ever — the gleaning law for the poor appended in the next verse.
22:
On Leviticus 23:22 — “And when ye reap the harvest of your land, thou shalt not make clean riddance of the corners of thy field… thou shalt leave them unto the poor, and to the stranger” — the witnesses mark the gleaning left for the poor.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the charity. Adam Clarke and Keil and Delitzsch refer to Leviticus 19:9, the same law repeated. Charles Ellicott: in the midst of rejoicing for the harvest, the Lawgiver bids them remember the poor. The Reformed Gill: repeated, the feast of weeks being the firstfruits of wheat, that they forget not God’s due. Wesley: from “ye” to “thou,” pressing the duty on every reaper.

The verse repeats the gleaning law. The corners and gleanings are left for the poor and the stranger. Thanksgiving overflows in charity.

So the witnesses see the law of gleaning set in the very midst of the harvest feast, that thanksgiving issue in kindness to the poor. The witnesses note the command: “thou shalt not make clean riddance of the corners of thy field… thou shalt leave them unto the poor, and to the stranger” — the reaper was not to reap the very edges of his field nor gather the dropped gleanings, but to leave them for the needy and the sojourner; the same law, the witnesses observe (Clarke, Keil), already given at Leviticus 19:9. They mark the fitness of its repetition here, which Gill and Ellicott draw out: at the feast of weeks, the firstfruits of the wheat harvest, when the people rejoiced in God’s bounty, they were the more to remember the poor, lest gratitude to God forget compassion to man; the Lawgiver sets charity in the midst of their thankfulness. They observe Wesley’s note on the change from the plural “ye reap” to the singular “thou shalt”: the duty is pressed home upon every individual reaper, that none plead exemption; each who has a harvest must leave his share for the poor. They note the principle the witnesses see: those whom God has blessed with plenty are stewards for the needy; the harvest is not wholly their own. They observe that true thanksgiving to God overflows in mercy to the poor and the stranger; the feast of firstfruits bears fruit in charity. They note that God provides for the poor through the bounty of the rich. The verse thus repeats the gleaning law: the corners left for the poor — the law turning next, in a new word, to the feast of trumpets.
23:
On Leviticus 23:23 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark the new word introducing the feast of trumpets.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the new festival of Leviticus 23:24–32 is introduced by a separate formula, marking a distinct divine communication. The Puritan Henry: the feast of trumpets on the first day of the seventh month, which had once been the first month, the civil new year.

The verse opens a new precept. God speaks again to give the law of the seventh-month feasts. The autumn solemnities begin.

So the witnesses see a fresh divine word open the laws of the seventh month, beginning with the feast of trumpets. The witnesses note the separate formula: “the LORD spake unto Moses” — Ellicott observes that this new festival is introduced by its own preface, marking it off as a distinct communication from the spring feasts just rehearsed; the autumn solemnities form a fresh group. They mark the turn in the year that Henry draws out: the feasts now to be given fell in the seventh month (Tisri), which, though seventh in the sacred reckoning that began with the Exodus, had once been the first month of the civil year, the old new year; so the seventh month opened with festal solemnity, the trumpets sounding in the new (civil) year. They observe the gathering weight of this month in the calendar: within its first three weeks fell the feast of trumpets, the great day of atonement, and the feast of tabernacles — a dense season of penitence, expiation, and joy, crowning the year’s worship. They note that God divides his revelation, giving each feast its own word, that its distinctness and importance be felt. They observe that the spring feasts (Passover, firstfruits, Pentecost) looked back to redemption and the harvest’s firstfruits, while the autumn feasts gather up the year in repentance, atonement, and the ingathering. They note that the new word prepares for the trumpets. The verse thus opens the new precept: the LORD speaks the law of the seventh month — the feast of trumpets commanded in the next verse.
24:
On Leviticus 23:24 — “In the seventh month, in the first day of the month, shall ye have a sabbath, a memorial of blowing of trumpets, an holy convocation” — the witnesses mark the feast of trumpets, with a question of its meaning.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the day. Adam Clarke: the feast of trumpets, on the first of Tisri (September), the beginning of the civil year. Charles Ellicott: literally “remembrance blowing” (Numbers 29:1). The Reformed Calvin wonders at the Jewish notion that it commemorated Isaac’s deliverance and the ram. Wesley: a sabbath solemnized with extraordinary blowing of trumpets, in all the cities.

The verse sets the feast of trumpets. The first of the seventh month is a sabbath, a memorial of trumpet-blowing. The new (civil) year is hailed with trumpets.

So the witnesses see the seventh month opened with a sabbath of trumpet-blasts, the feast of trumpets. The witnesses note the day and its rest: “in the first day of the month, shall ye have a sabbath… an holy convocation” — the first of Tisri, which (as Clarke notes) answered to September and began the civil year, was kept as a day of rest and sacred assembly. They mark its distinctive sign: “a memorial of blowing of trumpets” — Ellicott renders the Hebrew “remembrance blowing” (Numbers 29:1); Wesley notes the trumpets were blown not in the common way of every new moon but in an extraordinary manner, throughout all the cities of Israel, hailing the day. They observe the uncertainty the witnesses confess as to what exactly was “remembered” or meant by the trumpets: Calvin records, and marvels at, the Jewish tradition that the feast commemorated the deliverance of Isaac from his father’s knife and the ram caught in the thicket, which he finds a strange and groundless fancy; the simpler sense is that the trumpets called the people to remembrance before God, to summon them to the solemn season of atonement now at hand. They note that the trumpet, which gathered Israel and proclaimed God’s acts, fitly opened the holiest month. They observe the typology some draw: the trumpet that calls to repentance before the day of atonement foreshadows the gospel call and the last trumpet (1 Corinthians 15:52). The verse thus sets the feast of trumpets: a sabbath of trumpet-blasts — its offering and rest in the next verse.
25:
On Leviticus 23:25 — “Ye shall do no servile work therein: but ye shall offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the rest and offering of the feast of trumpets.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: with the exception of what was absolutely necessary, all handicraft and trade were stopped; and as the festival was also the new moon, a threefold sacrifice was offered (Numbers 29:1). The Reformed Gill: only such work as was necessary for dressing food, but no manual or mechanic labour.

The verse appoints rest and sacrifice. No servile work, but a fire offering to the LORD. The feast of trumpets is kept with rest and offering.

So the witnesses see the feast of trumpets observed, like the other festivals, with rest from labour and the offering of sacrifice. The witnesses note the rest: “ye shall do no servile work therein” — Gill and Ellicott explain that all ordinary handicraft, trade, and laborious work was stopped, only the necessary preparing of food being allowed; the day was set free from common toil for worship. They mark the sacrifice: “ye shall offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD” — the day was honoured with special burnt and sin offerings; Ellicott observes that, since the first of the month was also the new moon, the sacrifices of the day were threefold — the daily continual offering, the new-moon offering, and the offering proper to the feast of trumpets itself (Numbers 29:1). They note the gathering solemnity of the season: the trumpets and the offerings together called Israel to draw near to God at the opening of the holiest month, preparing them for the day of atonement now ten days off. They observe that the feast joined rest and sacrifice, as all the holy convocations did: the people freed from work and brought near by offering. They note that the trumpet-blown day was no idle holiday but a summons to worship and self-examination before the great fast. They observe that God appoints both the ceasing from labour and the drawing near in sacrifice. The verse thus appoints rest and offering: no servile work, a fire offering — before a new word, in the next verses, gives the day of atonement.
26:
On Leviticus 23:26 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark the new word introducing the day of atonement.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the same formula that introduced the feast of trumpets now introduces the laws about the day of atonement. The Reformed Gill: the phrase distinguishes one precept from another, the day of atonement from the feast of trumpets.

The verse opens a new precept. God speaks again to give the law of the day of atonement. The great fast is about to be set.

So the witnesses see a fresh divine word open the law of the day of atonement, the solemn fast of the seventh month. The witnesses note the separate formula: “the LORD spake unto Moses” — Gill and Ellicott observe that this preface, recurring before each feast, distinguishes the day of atonement from the feast of trumpets just given, marking it as its own distinct ordinance. They mark the place of this day in the calendar: it is the only one of all the appointed seasons that is a fast, not a feast — a day of affliction and humbling in the midst of the festal year; though here set briefly among the calendar of feasts, its full ritual was given earlier (Leviticus 16:29), and here it is rehearsed for the people’s observance. They observe the solemnity the fresh word lends it: God sets the great day of expiation apart by its own command, that its weight be felt; on it the high priest entered the holiest with blood, and the sins of the whole nation were atoned. They note that the day of atonement stands at the heart of the autumn season, between the trumpets that summoned to repentance and the tabernacles that crowned the year with joy; first the call, then the cleansing, then the rejoicing. They observe the typology the witnesses carry: this day above all prefigures the atoning work of Christ, who by his own blood entered once into the holy place (Hebrews 9:12). They note that God gives the law of atonement its own solemn opening. The verse thus opens the new precept: the LORD speaks the law of the day of atonement — its date and demand following in the next verse.
27:
On Leviticus 23:27 — “Also on the tenth day of this seventh month there shall be a day of atonement… and ye shall afflict your souls, and offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the day of atonement and the afflicting of souls.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the fast. Keil and Delitzsch: a day of atonement, a holy convocation; afflict your souls. Charles Ellicott: “afflict your souls” means fast (Leviticus 16:29). The Reformed Calvin: the word for atonement, in the plural, signifies the appeasing of God by expiation. Wesley: afflict your souls with fasting and bitter repentance for all sins, especially the golden calf.

The verse sets the day of atonement. On the tenth, a holy convocation, the people afflict their souls and offer by fire. Expiation and humbling mark the day.

So the witnesses see the great day of atonement appointed, with fasting and sacrifice for the sins of all the people. The witnesses note the date and holiness: “on the tenth day of this seventh month… a holy convocation” — the tenth of Tisri, kept as a sacred assembly. They mark the demand peculiar to this day: “ye shall afflict your souls” — Ellicott and the witnesses explain this as fasting and self-humbling, the only fast in the calendar; Wesley adds that it was a day of bitter repentance for all their sins, and notes the tradition that God would have them especially remember the sin of the golden calf, which he had threatened to visit. They observe the sacrifice joined to the fast: “an offering made by fire unto the LORD” — the special offerings of the day (Numbers 29:8), and (by the fuller ritual of Leviticus 16:1) the high priest’s entering the holiest with blood. They note Calvin’s remark on the word “atonement” (in the Hebrew a plural, “atonements”): it signifies the appeasing of God and the blotting out of guilt by expiation, the plural marking the fulness and completeness of the cleansing. They observe the union of the two things: humbling within (the afflicted soul) and atonement without (the offered blood); true repentance and the appointed sacrifice meet on this day. They note the typology the witnesses press: the day of atonement is the clearest Old Testament figure of Christ’s expiation, the afflicted soul answering to the repentance that receives him. The verse thus sets the day of atonement: the soul afflicted, the offering made — with the warning, in the next verses, against any work or any failure to fast.
28:
On Leviticus 23:28 — “And ye shall do no work in that same day: for it is a day of atonement, to make an atonement for you before the LORD your God” — the witnesses mark the entire rest of the day of atonement.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the strictness. Adam Clarke refers to the full account at Leviticus 16:2. Charles Ellicott: “no manner of work” — this is the only day, besides the weekly Sabbath, kept with total rest. The Reformed Gill: no work, no more than on the weekly sabbath. Wesley: signifying the necessity of repentance and forgiveness, and the desperate state of the impenitent.

The verse forbids all work. No work at all on the day of atonement, for it makes atonement before God. The day is kept as a total rest.

So the witnesses see the day of atonement marked off by a total cessation of work, like the weekly Sabbath. The witnesses note the strict rest: “ye shall do no work in that same day” — not merely the abstinence from servile work commanded on the festival days, but, as Ellicott and Gill observe, a complete rest from all manner of work, such as the weekly Sabbath required; the day of atonement and the Sabbath alone were kept with this entire ceasing. They mark the reason given: “for it is a day of atonement, to make an atonement for you before the LORD your God” — the day was wholly given to the great work of expiation, and the people were to do nothing of their own, that all might be occupied with the atonement made for them; their rest from work answered to the truth that the atonement was God’s work, not theirs. They observe Wesley’s reflection on the meaning: by so solemn and total a day God would signify the absolute necessity every man has of repentance and forgiveness of sin, and the desperate condition of all who remain impenitent. They note that the full ritual of the day — the high priest, the two goats, the blood within the veil — was given at Leviticus 16:2, to which Clarke refers. They observe the typology: the people resting while atonement is made figures the believer’s resting from his own works to receive the finished atonement of Christ. They note that on this day Israel did nothing but be atoned for. The verse thus forbids all work: total rest, for atonement is made — the penalty for breaking the fast following in the next verse.
29:
On Leviticus 23:29 — “For whatsoever soul it be that shall not be afflicted in that same day, he shall be cut off from among his people” — the witnesses mark the penalty for not fasting.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the severity. Charles Ellicott: any member of the community who does not fast on this day is to be cut off (Leviticus 19:8). The Reformed Gill: which can fast and does not; the sick and children under nine excepted. Calvin: learn how greatly the sacrifice of a humbled heart pleases God, since so severe a punishment is set for contempt of this ceremony.

The verse threatens the careless. Whoever is not afflicted that day shall be cut off. The fast is bound on every soul.

So the witnesses see the afflicting of the soul on the day of atonement enforced under the gravest penalty. The witnesses note the threat: “whatsoever soul… shall not be afflicted in that same day, he shall be cut off from among his people” — whoever did not humble himself and fast on the day of atonement was to be cut off, excluded from the covenant people; Ellicott takes this of the divine excision threatened elsewhere (Leviticus 19:8). They mark the reasonable limits the tradition set, which Gill records: the law bound everyone who could fast and would not; the sick, and children under nine years, were not held to it, the affliction being required only of those able to bear it. They observe the lesson Calvin draws from so severe a sanction: we may learn from it how greatly the sacrifice of an afflicted and humbled heart pleases God, since he threatens so heavy a punishment for the mere contempt of this ceremony of humbling; God prizes the broken and contrite spirit. They note the spiritual point the witnesses press: the outward fast stood for the inward humbling, and to refuse it was to refuse repentance, to despise the atonement, and so to cut oneself off from the people whose sins were being atoned. They observe that he who will not afflict his soul for sin has no part in the cleansing; impenitence excludes from grace. They note that God will have his people humbled before him on the day of their atonement. The verse thus threatens the careless: cut off if not afflicted — the penalty extended, in the next verse, to any who work.
30:
On Leviticus 23:30 — “And whatsoever soul it be that doeth any work in that same day, the same soul will I destroy from among his people” — the witnesses mark the penalty for working on the day.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: this is the only festival to be kept like the sabbath, with total rest; whereas elsewhere God threatens the offender with being “cut off,” here he says “I will destroy.” The Reformed Gill: any sort of work whatever, the day to be kept as strictly as the weekly sabbath.

The verse threatens the worker. Whoever does any work that day God will destroy. The total rest is guarded by the gravest threat.

So the witnesses see the rest of the day of atonement enforced, like the fast, under the severest sanction. The witnesses note the threat: “whatsoever soul… that doeth any work in that same day, the same soul will I destroy” — to do any work on the day of atonement was to incur destruction from among the people; Gill stresses “any sort of work whatever,” the day to be kept as strictly as, or more strictly than, the weekly Sabbath. They mark Ellicott’s observation on the heightened language: whereas in most cases of such breach God threatens that the offender shall be “cut off,” here he says with peculiar force, “I will destroy him” — God himself undertaking the punishment, a sign of the singular sanctity of the day. They observe the doubling of the penalties (v. 29 against the unafflicted, v. 30 against the worker): both the fast and the rest were guarded by the threat of destruction, that nothing might profane the day of atonement. They note the reason the witnesses see: on this one day above all, when atonement was being made for the whole nation, the people were to do nothing but be still and be atoned for; to work was to act as though the atonement were nothing, to thrust oneself out of the cleansing. They observe that God claims the day wholly to himself and his expiating work. They note that the very strictness preaches the weight of atonement and the danger of slighting it. The verse thus threatens the worker: “I will destroy” the worker on that day — the prohibition repeated yet again in the next verse.
31:
On Leviticus 23:31 — “Ye shall do no manner of work: it shall be a statute for ever throughout your generations in all your dwellings” — the witnesses mark the repeated and perpetual command of rest.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: owing to the great sanctity of the day, the command to abstain from all work is repeated after the penalty, to impress it the more; a statute for ever. The Reformed Gill: repeated, that it might be observed, and to show how strictly God required this day to be kept.

The verse repeats the rest. No manner of work, a statute for ever in every dwelling. The command is pressed home and made perpetual.

So the witnesses see the command of rest on the day of atonement repeated and made perpetual, the more to impress it. The witnesses note the renewed prohibition: “ye shall do no manner of work” — the rule, already given (vv. 28, 30), is here stated again; Gill observes it is repeated that it might be the more surely observed, and to show how strictly God required this day to be kept and how careful men should be not to break it. They mark Ellicott’s point that, owing to the great sanctity of the day, the command to abstain from all work is set down once more, after the penalty has been declared, in order to impress it the more effectually upon the people; the repetition is a measure of the day’s solemnity. They observe the perpetuity and universality: “a statute for ever throughout your generations in all your dwellings” — the law binds not one generation only but all, and not at the sanctuary only but in every Israelite dwelling, wherever they lived. They note that God lays great weight on this day’s rest, guarding it with penalty and pressing it with repetition; the entire ceasing from work was no light matter but the very mark of the day. They observe that the perpetual statute carried the day of atonement down all Israel’s history, the annual reminder of sin and its expiation. They note that what God repeats, his people should the more diligently keep. The verse thus repeats the rest: no manner of work, a statute for ever — the bounds of the day, evening to evening, fixed in the next verse.
32:
On Leviticus 23:32 — “It shall be unto you a sabbath of rest, and ye shall afflict your souls: in the ninth day of the month at even, from even unto even, shall ye celebrate your sabbath” — the witnesses mark the bounds of the day, evening to evening.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “a sabbath of rest” (as in Leviticus 16:31). The Reformed Gill refers to the sabbath of rest. Wesley: it began at the evening of the ninth day and continued till the evening of the tenth; called “your sabbath.”

The verse fixes the bounds. A sabbath of rest with affliction, kept from evening to evening. The day is measured from the ninth at even.

So the witnesses see the exact span of the day of atonement laid down, a sabbath of rest reckoned from evening to evening. The witnesses note the double character: “a sabbath of rest, and ye shall afflict your souls” — the day was at once a sabbath (total rest from work) and a fast (the afflicting of the soul); rest and humbling joined, the body still and the heart bowed. They mark the bounds: “in the ninth day of the month at even, from even unto even” — Wesley explains that the observance began at the evening of the ninth day and continued until the evening of the tenth, a full day reckoned, after the Hebrew manner, from sunset to sunset; the fast was thus kept entire, evening to evening. They observe Ellicott’s note that the phrase “a sabbath of rest” is the same used of the day at Leviticus 16:31, marking its kinship with the weekly Sabbath in its total rest. They note the name “your sabbath,” which Wesley observes may mark it as peculiarly theirs, the great day of their atonement and rest. They observe that the precise bounding of the day ensured the whole was kept, none of it profaned at either end; the fast and rest were complete. They note that God measures the times of his worship exactly. They observe that the day of atonement is the soul’s sabbath, resting from sin in the atonement made. The verse thus fixes the bounds: a sabbath of rest, evening to evening — before a new word, in the next verses, gives the feast of tabernacles.
33:
On Leviticus 23:33 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark the new word introducing the feast of tabernacles.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott: the feast of tabernacles, like the feasts of new year and atonement, is introduced by its own special formula. The Puritan Henry: one of the three great feasts at which all the males must attend, celebrated with more joy than any other.

The verse opens a new precept. God speaks again to give the law of the feast of tabernacles. The last and gladdest feast begins.

So the witnesses see a fresh divine word open the law of the feast of tabernacles, the joyful close of the year’s feasts. The witnesses note the separate formula: “the LORD spake unto Moses” — Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott observe that the feast of tabernacles, like the feasts of new year (trumpets) and atonement, is introduced by its own special preface, marking it as a distinct ordinance and the last of the seventh-month solemnities. They mark the eminence of this feast that Henry draws out: it was one of the three great pilgrim feasts (with Passover and Pentecost) at which all the males of Israel were bound to appear before the LORD, and it was celebrated with more expressions of joy and gladness than any other — the most festal of all the feasts. They observe its place in the season: coming just five days after the solemn fast of atonement, it turned the year’s worship from humbling to rejoicing; the cleansing past, the people kept holiday before God. They note its double ground (to be unfolded in the verses following): it was the feast of ingathering, the harvest-home at the year’s end (Exodus 23:16), and a memorial of the wilderness, when Israel dwelt in booths. They observe the typology the witnesses carry: the feast of God dwelling with his people in tents looks on to the Word made flesh, who “tabernacled” among us (John 1:14), and to the final rest when God shall dwell with men. They note that the gladdest feast crowns the year. The verse thus opens the new precept: the LORD speaks the law of tabernacles — its date and length in the next verse.
34:
On Leviticus 23:34 — “The fifteenth day of this seventh month shall be the feast of tabernacles for seven days unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the feast of tabernacles set.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the date and meaning. Adam Clarke: in this feast the people left their houses and dwelt in booths of branches. Charles Ellicott: the fifteenth of Tishri, only four days after the day of atonement. The Reformed Calvin: instituted that they might remember they dwelt in tents in the desert, having no fixed home. Wesley: of tents or booths, to remind them of the wilderness and stir them to bless God.

The verse sets the feast. The fifteenth of the seventh month begins the seven-day feast of tabernacles. The people are to dwell in booths.

So the witnesses see the feast of tabernacles appointed, when Israel left their houses to dwell in booths for seven days. The witnesses note the date: “the fifteenth day of this seventh month” — Ellicott observes it fell only four days after the day of atonement, so that the solemn fast was swiftly followed by the gladdest feast; the people, newly cleansed, kept festival before God. They mark the manner of the feast: the people, as Clarke and Wesley note, left their settled houses and dwelt for the week in booths or tents made of branches, in the open air. They observe the twofold meaning the witnesses give it. Calvin and Wesley press the memorial sense: the booths were to remind Israel that their fathers dwelt in tents in the wilderness, having no fixed dwelling-place but wandering as pilgrims, kept and protected by God; the feast stirred them to bless God for that gracious keeping and for the settled land they now enjoyed. They note (to be unfolded below) the other ground, the ingathering of harvest, the feast falling when the fruits of the year were gathered in. They observe the typology the witnesses carry: the dwelling in booths figures the pilgrim life of God’s people on earth, who have here no continuing city; and the feast of God tabernacling with Israel looks on to Christ, who “dwelt” (tabernacled) among us (John 1:14). They note that the feast is “unto the LORD,” kept to his honour. The verse thus sets the feast: tabernacles, seven days from the fifteenth — the first day, the next verse says, a holy convocation.
35:
On Leviticus 23:35 — “On the first day shall be an holy convocation: ye shall do no servile work therein” — the witnesses mark the first day’s holy convocation.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes the second-Temple custom: at daybreak a priest with a golden pitcher went to the pool of Siloam and drew water (the water-pouring, cf. John 7:37). The Reformed Gill: a day for the people to be called to holy exercises, prayer, praise, and the reading of the law.

The verse hallows the first day. It is a holy convocation, free of servile work. The feast opens with a sacred assembly.

So the witnesses see the first day of tabernacles kept as a holy convocation, with rest from common labour. The witnesses note the sanctity: “on the first day shall be an holy convocation” — Gill observes the people were called together to holy exercises, to prayer, praise, and the reading of the law; the joyful feast opened with worship. They mark the rest commanded: “ye shall do no servile work therein” — as on the other festival days, laborious work was laid aside, though the needful dressing of food was allowed; the day was freed for the things of God. They observe the rich detail Ellicott records of the second-Temple observance of this feast, especially the ceremony of the water-pouring: at daybreak a priest, with a jubilant procession and music, went with a golden pitcher to the pool of Siloam, drew water, and poured it out at the altar — a rite of thanksgiving and prayer for rain, to which (the witnesses note) our Lord alluded when on “the great day of the feast” he cried, “If any man thirst, let him come unto me and drink” (John 7:37). They note that the feast of tabernacles, though the gladdest, was no less framed by holy convocations than the rest; joy and worship went together. They observe that the people’s rejoicing was hallowed by being kept before the LORD. They note that the assembly gathered Israel to praise the God of the harvest and of the wilderness. The verse thus hallows the first day: a holy convocation, free of servile work — the seven days’ offerings and the eighth day following in the next verse.
36:
