On Leviticus 23:36 — “Seven days ye shall offer an offering made by fire… on the eighth day shall be an holy convocation… it is a solemn assembly; and ye shall do no servile work therein” — the witnesses mark the seven days’ offerings and the eighth-day assembly.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the special sacrifices were thirteen bullocks, two rams, fourteen lambs, with a goat for a sin offering (Numbers 29:13). The Reformed Calvin: they kept holiday on the first and eighth days, dwelling in booths the seven. Wesley: the eighth day, though not strictly of the feast, belonged to it in a larger sense, called “the great day of the feast” (John 7:37).

The verse appoints the offerings and the eighth day. Fire offerings seven days, the eighth a solemn assembly. The feast is crowned with a great closing day.

So the witnesses see the seven days of tabernacles filled with abundant sacrifice and crowned by a solemn eighth-day assembly. The witnesses note the offerings: “seven days ye shall offer an offering made by fire” — the sacrifices of this feast were the most lavish of all, Ellicott reckoning them (from Numbers) at thirteen bullocks on the first day, with two rams and fourteen lambs and a goat for sin, the bullocks decreasing by one each day (Numbers 29:13); the gladdest feast was the richest in offering. They mark the eighth day: “on the eighth day shall be an holy convocation… a solemn assembly” — Calvin notes they kept holiday on the first and eighth days, dwelling in booths the seven between; and Wesley observes that the eighth day, though strictly a separate festival and not one of the seven booth-days, belonged to the feast in a larger sense and was called “the great day of the feast,” the day on which our Lord cried out the invitation to the thirsty (John 7:37). They observe the rest: “ye shall do no servile work therein” — both the first and eighth days were holy convocations of rest. They note the fulness of the feast: a week of dwelling in booths, daily sacrifice, and two holy days, opening and closing the most joyful season of Israel’s year. They observe that the eighth day, beyond the seven, may figure the eternal rest and joy that lie beyond this world’s pilgrimage. They note that the feast ends in solemn gladness before God. The verse thus appoints the offerings and the eighth day: the feast crowned with a great assembly — the summary of all the feasts following in the next verse.
37:
On Leviticus 23:37 — “These are the feasts of the LORD, which ye shall proclaim to be holy convocations, to offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD… every thing upon his day” — the witnesses mark the summary of the feasts.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the above-named six festivals — Passover, Pentecost, new year, atonement, tabernacles (besides the Sabbath). The Reformed Gill: besides the sabbath, the passover, unleavened bread, Pentecost, trumpets, atonement, and tabernacles. Wesley: “a sacrifice” — a sin offering, called by the general name, for the principal end of all sacrifices was the expiation of sin.

The verse sums the feasts. These are the LORD’s feasts, each with its offering on its day. The calendar is gathered into one.

So the witnesses see the whole calendar of yearly feasts gathered into a closing summary. The witnesses note the recapitulation: “these are the feasts of the LORD” — Ellicott and Gill list the festivals here summed up: the Passover and unleavened bread, the feast of weeks (Pentecost), the feast of trumpets (new year), the day of atonement, and the feast of tabernacles — the appointed seasons of the year, besides the weekly Sabbath. They mark the common element drawn out: “to offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD… every thing upon his day” — each feast had its own proper sacrifices, to be offered each on its appointed day; the worship of every season was fixed and ordered, nothing left to chance or to man’s choosing. They observe Wesley’s note on the kinds of offering gathered under the term: burnt offerings, meat offerings, sacrifices (especially the sin offering, called by the general name because the chief end of all sacrifice was the expiation of sin), drink offerings — the whole apparatus of worship distributed through the year. They note the order and fitness the witnesses see: God appointed for each holy day its own offerings, that his people might serve him in his own way, at his own times; the calendar was a complete and ordered system of worship. They observe that the feasts together rehearsed the whole gospel in shadow — redemption, firstfruits, the Spirit, atonement, and God dwelling with his people. They note that all is “unto the LORD.” The verse thus sums the feasts: the LORD’s seasons, each with its offering — distinguished, in the next verse, from the Sabbath and the freewill gifts.
38:
On Leviticus 23:38 — “Beside the sabbaths of the LORD, and beside your gifts, and beside all your vows, and beside all your freewill offerings” — the witnesses mark the feasts distinguished from the Sabbath and freewill gifts.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: by metonymy “sabbaths” stands for the sacrifices of the sabbaths. The Reformed Gill: the seventh-day sabbaths, whose observance the feasts did not hinder. Wesley: God will not have any sabbath-sacrifice diminished because of the addition of the festival offerings.

The verse distinguishes the feasts. They are besides the sabbaths, gifts, vows, and freewill offerings. The festal offerings are added, not substituted.

So the witnesses see the feast offerings set apart from, and added to, the regular Sabbath sacrifices and the people’s voluntary gifts. The witnesses note the distinction: “beside the sabbaths of the LORD” — the offerings of the yearly feasts were over and above the sacrifices of the weekly Sabbath; Gill observes that when a feast fell on a Sabbath, it did not set aside the Sabbath’s own observance or offerings. They mark Ellicott’s note that “sabbaths” here stands, by a figure of speech (metonymy), for the sacrifices proper to the sabbaths; the feast sacrifices were additional to these. They observe Wesley’s point of principle: God would not have any sabbath-sacrifice diminished because of the addition of the festival offerings; the one was not to crowd out the other, but each rendered in full. They note the further additions named: “your gifts… your vows… your freewill offerings” — the feast offerings were also distinct from the people’s voluntary and occasional sacrifices, their freewill gifts, the payment of their vows, and their thank offerings; all these continued alongside the fixed festal worship. They observe the fulness of Israel’s service: the appointed feasts did not absorb or replace the ordinary and the voluntary worship, but were added to it; God’s due was rendered in every kind. They note that abundant worship is not lessened by being various. They observe that the freewill offerings show that love added its own gifts to the law’s appointments. The verse thus distinguishes the feasts: besides the sabbaths and the freewill gifts — before the law returns, in the next verses, to the feast of tabernacles.
39:
On Leviticus 23:39 — “Also in the fifteenth day of the seventh month, when ye have gathered in the fruit of the land, ye shall keep a feast unto the LORD seven days” — the witnesses mark the feast of tabernacles as the ingathering.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott: the law now recurs to the feast of tabernacles, with supplementary regulations; on the first and eighth days a sabbath. The Reformed Gill: another end of the feast, to give thanks for the ingathering of the fruits. Wesley: a repetition with fuller explanation of the manner and reason; the fruit, not the corn (gathered before), but the wine and oil.

The verse adds the ingathering. After the harvest is gathered, they keep the seven-day feast. Tabernacles is the harvest-home of the year.

So the witnesses see the feast of tabernacles set forth again, now as the feast of ingathering at the year’s end. The witnesses note the harvest occasion: “when ye have gathered in the fruit of the land” — the feast fell after the whole produce of the year was gathered; Wesley distinguishes that this “fruit” means not the corn (long since reaped before Pentecost) but the later fruits, the vintage and the oil, now gathered in; the feast was the harvest-home, the joyful close of the agricultural year. They mark that the law here recurs to tabernacles after the summary of all the feasts (vv. 37–38): Ellicott and Keil note these closing verses are supplementary, adding the reason of the feast and the rite of the booths and branches not given above. They observe the double significance now complete: the feast was both the ingathering, thanking God for the year’s fruits, and (as the following verses show) the memorial of the wilderness booths; gratitude for present plenty joined to remembrance of past pilgrimage. They note Gill’s point that another and distinct end of the feast is here remarked, the giving of thanks for the harvest gathered. They observe the fitness of rejoicing: with barns and presses full, the people kept their gladdest feast, owning God as the giver of all increase. They note the typology: the ingathering feast looks on to the great harvest-home at the end of the world, when God’s people are gathered in. They observe that thanksgiving crowns the year. The verse thus adds the ingathering: tabernacles the harvest-home — the booths and branches commanded in the next verses.
40:
On Leviticus 23:40 — “And ye shall take you on the first day the boughs of goodly trees, branches of palm trees, and the boughs of thick trees, and willows of the brook; and ye shall rejoice before the LORD your God seven days” — the witnesses mark the branches and the rejoicing.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the trees. Adam Clarke: the Jews and many critics take the “goodly tree” for the citron, the “thick tree” for the myrtle. Charles Ellicott: the four species minutely defined in the second Temple. Wesley: olive, myrtle, pine, and willows (Nehemiah 8:15). The Reformed Calvin: by this symbol they were taught to keep the day with joy and gladness.

The verse commands the boughs. They take goodly boughs, palm, thick branches, and willows, and rejoice seven days. The feast is kept with branches and joy.

So the witnesses see the people take branches of trees and keep the feast of tabernacles with seven days of rejoicing before God. The witnesses note the boughs: “the boughs of goodly trees, branches of palm trees, and the boughs of thick trees, and willows of the brook” — the expositors discuss which trees are meant: Clarke notes the common Jewish view that the “goodly tree” is the citron and the “thick tree” the myrtle; Wesley names olive, myrtle, pine, and willow, after Nehemiah 8:15; Ellicott observes the four species were minutely defined in the second-Temple practice. They mark the use of the branches: they served to build and adorn the booths, and (in later custom) were carried and waved in the festal processions. They observe the command to rejoice: “ye shall rejoice before the LORD your God seven days” — Calvin notes that by the very symbol of the green branches the people were taught to celebrate the day with joy and gladness; this was the feast of holy mirth, the gladdest of the year, kept in God’s presence. They note that the rejoicing was commanded, a duty as well as a delight: God would have his people glad before him, thankful for his mercies in the wilderness and in the harvest. They observe the fitness of the green boughs to a feast of thanksgiving for the fruits of the land. They note the typology some draw: the palms and rejoicing look on to the great multitude with palms before the throne (Revelation 7:9). The verse thus commands the boughs: branches and seven days’ joy — the keeping of the feast yearly enjoined in the next verse.
41:
On Leviticus 23:41 — “And ye shall keep it a feast unto the LORD seven days in the year. It shall be a statute for ever in your generations: ye shall celebrate it in the seventh month” — the witnesses mark the yearly perpetual keeping.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the seven days denote the feast of tabernacles proper, the eighth being the concluding festival (Leviticus 23:36); “in your generations,” throughout the ages. The Reformed Gill: every year, seven days, from the fifteenth to the twenty-second of Tisri.

The verse fixes the yearly feast. It is kept seven days every year, a perpetual statute, in the seventh month. The feast is made annual and lasting.

So the witnesses see the feast of tabernacles bound on Israel as a yearly and perpetual observance. The witnesses note the annual keeping: “ye shall keep it a feast unto the LORD seven days in the year” — Gill specifies the span, every year from the fifteenth to the twenty-second of the seventh month (Tisri); the feast was to recur each year at its fixed season. They mark Ellicott’s distinction that the “seven days” here denote the feast of tabernacles proper (the booth-dwelling days), while the eighth day named earlier (Leviticus 23:36) was the separate concluding festival; the seven and the eighth together made the full season. They observe the perpetuity: “a statute for ever in your generations” — binding throughout the ages of Israel, not for that generation only; Ellicott renders it “throughout your generations.” They note the fixed time: “ye shall celebrate it in the seventh month” — kept always in its appointed month, neither shifted nor forgotten. They observe that God secures the memory of his mercies by making the feast annual and perpetual; each generation in turn kept the ingathering and the booths, and so owned both the harvest and the wilderness keeping as its own. They note that the lasting statute carried the feast down all Israel’s history, to be kept with joy in every age. They observe that perpetual worship answers to perpetual mercy. The verse thus fixes the yearly feast: seven days every year, for ever — the dwelling in booths commanded again in the next verses.
42:
On Leviticus 23:42 — “Ye shall dwell in booths seven days; all that are Israelites born shall dwell in booths” — the witnesses mark the dwelling in booths.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: seven days, because the eighth was a separate festival when the booths were no more used (Leviticus 23:36). The Reformed Gill: not obliged to dwell in them on the eighth day. Wesley: booths erected in their cities, streets, gardens, or flat housetops.

The verse commands the booths. All native Israelites dwell in booths seven days. The pilgrim dwelling is kept.

So the witnesses see the command that all Israel dwell in booths for the seven days of the feast. The witnesses note the requirement: “ye shall dwell in booths seven days; all that are Israelites born shall dwell in booths” — for the week of the feast the people left their houses and lodged in temporary booths, a duty laid on all the native-born of Israel. They mark the places the booths were set, which Wesley records: in their cities and towns, in the streets, the gardens, or on the flat roofs of their houses, made of branches and boughs. They observe the limit Ellicott and Gill note: the dwelling in booths was for the seven days only, not the eighth, since the eighth day was a separate concluding festival when the booths were no longer used (Leviticus 23:36). They observe the meaning the witnesses keep before us: by leaving their settled homes for frail booths, the people were made to feel and remember the unsettled, pilgrim life of their fathers in the wilderness, and to own that even their fixed dwellings and the land itself were God’s gift. They note the lesson the witnesses draw for all generations: the booth-dwelling preaches that God’s people are pilgrims and strangers on the earth, whose true and abiding home is with God; the present dwelling is but a tent. They observe that the rich and settled were thus yearly reminded of dependence and pilgrimage. They note the typology: God dwelling with his people in booths looks on to his dwelling with them for ever. The verse thus commands the booths: all Israel in booths seven days — the reason of it given in the next verse.
43:
On Leviticus 23:43 — “That your generations may know that I made the children of Israel to dwell in booths, when I brought them out of the land of Egypt: I am the LORD your God” — the witnesses mark the reason of the feast.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: God had two objects — to perpetuate the display of his providence and grace in bringing them out of Egypt, and in preserving them in the wilderness. Charles Ellicott: when their posterity securely occupy Canaan, the yearly booth may remind them of God’s goodness. The Reformed Gill: the Israelites were obliged by providence to make booths to dwell in.

The verse gives the reason. That their children may know God made Israel dwell in booths at the Exodus. The feast preserves the memory of the wilderness.

So the witnesses see the reason of the booth-dwelling declared: the teaching of later generations about God’s keeping in the wilderness. The witnesses note the purpose: “that your generations may know that I made the children of Israel to dwell in booths” — the yearly keeping of the feast was to preserve, for children who had never seen the desert, the memory of how their fathers, brought out of Egypt, dwelt in booths and tents in the wilderness, with no settled home, kept and provided for by God alone. They mark Clarke’s summary of the two objects God had in view: to perpetuate the remembrance of the wonderful display of his providence and grace, both in bringing Israel out of Egypt and in preserving them through the wilderness years. They observe Ellicott’s note on the contrast that gave the feast its force: when the posterity of Israel were securely settled in the good land of Canaan, dwelling in solid houses amid plenty, the yearly return to the frail booth would remind them that it was not always so, and that all they had was of God’s goodness. They observe the seal set on it: “I am the LORD your God” — the whole grounded in his covenant authority and faithfulness. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: God would have his mercies remembered across the generations, the settled and prosperous taught their dependence by a yearly pilgrimage to the booth. They observe that the feast was a standing testimony to redemption and providence. The verse thus gives the reason: that the generations may know the wilderness keeping — closing, in the next verse, with Moses’ declaring of the feasts.
44:
On Leviticus 23:44 — “And Moses declared unto the children of Israel the feasts of the LORD” — the witnesses mark Moses’ faithful declaring of the feasts.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: in accordance with the command he received (Leviticus 23:2), Moses explained to Israel the number and motive of the festivals; the verse forms a fitting conclusion. The Reformed Gill: the several feasts before recited, their order, manner, and time. Wesley: we have reason to be thankful that the feasts now are fewer, less burdensome, more spiritual and significant.

The verse closes the chapter. Moses declares the feasts of the LORD to Israel. The calendar is faithfully delivered.

So the witnesses see Moses faithfully deliver to Israel the whole calendar of the feasts as God had commanded. The witnesses note the obedience: “Moses declared unto the children of Israel the feasts of the LORD” — Ellicott observes that, in accordance with the charge he received at the chapter’s opening (Leviticus 23:2), Moses now made known to the people the number, order, manner, time, and motive of the festivals; the verse forms an appropriate conclusion to the whole. They mark Gill’s summary that Moses set before them the several feasts before recited — the Passover and unleavened bread, firstfruits, Pentecost, trumpets, atonement, and tabernacles — with the order of them and the way and season of their keeping. They observe the faithfulness of the mediator: what God spoke to Moses, Moses declared to Israel, holding nothing back; the steward delivered his Lord’s whole message. They observe Wesley’s thankful reflection at the close: the people of God now are not bound to feasts so numerous, nor to an observance so burdensome and costly, but to fewer, more spiritual and significant seasons, surer and sweeter earnests of the rest to come; the shadows have given way to the substance in Christ. They note that the chapter, a complete calendar of grace, was thus handed to the people to be kept. They observe that all the feasts together rehearsed, in shadow, the work of redemption fulfilled in Christ. The verse thus closes the chapter: Moses declares the feasts of the LORD — the calendar faithfully given to Israel.
